name: Playwright
scope: A person with INTERMEDIATE proficiency level in Playwright as a competency should be able to / is capable of explaining and leveraging Playwright’s Node-runner→driver→browser-context→page multi-process architecture, auto-wait model and event loop; designing deterministic tests in TypeScript/ESM with Playwright Test runner, lifecycle hooks, project matrix, worker processes and advanced assertions (`expect`, `expect.poll`). They craft complex user flows (multi-tab, multi-origin, iframes, file upload/download, dialogs, geolocation, permissions, storage) using the full locator system (text/role/ARIA, chained filters, strict mode, custom selector engines) and proper waits (`waitForURL`, `waitForRequest`, events) while encapsulating steps via Page-Object/Screenplay patterns, reusable fixtures (auth, data seeding, scoped test/worker), parameterisation (`test.use`, tags, skip/fixme) and visual/trace/video capture. They launch and configure Chromium, Firefox, WebKit, Chrome/Edge channels in headful/headless modes, emulate mobile devices with `playwright.devices`, control proxies, HTTP auth, SSL, CORS and media queries, and manage context isolation. They intercept, stub or mutate network/WebSocket traffic with `route`, HAR, inline mocks, conditional pass-through, and couple UI steps with API validation using `APIRequestContext`. They optimise reliability and speed via parallel workers, sharding, retries, selective video/trace, resource-aware CI containers, flake dashboards and execution metrics. They debug with `pw` CLI, VS Code extension, Trace Viewer, HTML/JUnit/Allure reporters, `DEBUG=pw:api` logs and headful `--debug` UI, analysing timelines, console output and snapshots. They integrate suites into GitHub Actions, Azure Pipelines, GitLab, Jenkins, CircleCI etc., cache browsers with `npx playwright install --with-deps`, orchestrate service containers, publish artefacts, enforce thresholds and branch-gating. They maintain code quality through ESLint, Prettier, strict TypeScript, SOLID abstractions, private npm helpers, version upgrades and meaningful code reviews, while embedding accessibility checks (axe-core, ARIA roles), security headers, CSP, secret redaction and GDPR compliance. They interface with BDD layers (Cucumber, Codecept), visual regression services (Percy, Applitools, Loki), test management (Zephyr, Xray), cloud labs (BrowserStack, Sauce, LambdaTest) and track emerging features like Component Testing, experimental UI or AI test generators. They routinely deliver suites <10 minutes for ~500 tests, sustain <2 % flake rate, ≥80 % critical-path coverage, produce actionable trace/video/log bundles for each defect and mentor juniors on locators, waits, fixtures and CI tuning.
long_scope:
COMPETENCY SCOPE  
Playwright – INTERMEDIATE (≈ 3-5 years focused use in production-grade automation)

1. Core Architectural & Conceptual Fluency  
   • Understand how Playwright’s multi-process architecture (Node runner ⇆ driver ⇆ browser contexts ⇆ pages ⇆ CDP/WebSocket) differs from Selenium/WebDriver and the benefits it brings (auto-waits, isolation, deterministic events).  
   • Explain the life-cycle of a test run: test runner bootstrap → project matrix expansion → worker process spawning → browser launch → context creation → page orchestration → teardown hooks.  
   • Grasp Playwright’s event-driven model; know when callbacks, listeners, async/await and promises are resolved inside the driver layer.  
   • Be fluent in the locator paradigm: hierarchical queries, text/role/ARIA selectors, chained filters, strict mode, `getBy*` queries, custom selector engines—understand internal polling vs manual waits and when each is triggered.  
   • Recognise Playwright Test as opinionated runner vs library-only mode; enumerate the components (configuration, CLI, reporters, trace viewer, test generator).  

2. Test Design & Authoring Capabilities  
   • Compose readable, deterministic tests using TypeScript (and/or modern ES modules in JS).  
   • Model complex user flows: multi-tab, multi-origin (browser context isolation), iFrame handling, file uploads, downloads, dialogs, geolocation, permissions, clipboard, cookies, web storage.  
   • Use assertions from `@playwright/test` for DOM, API and expect-poll patterns; know when to prefer `expect(locator).toHaveX` auto-retry vs `await expect.poll`.  
   • Employ built-in waits (`waitForURL`, `waitForRequest`, `waitForEvent`) appropriately; avoid anti-patterns like `setTimeout`.  
   • Build and reuse fixtures: authentication, test data seeding, context-level vs page-level, auto vs manual, scoping (test / worker).  
   • Structure projects by Page Object (or Screenplay, Component, StepObject) patterns; advocate code deduplication and single-responsibility.  
   • Parameterise tests using `test.describe.configure`, `test.fixme`, `test.skip`, `test.use`, tag filters.  
   • Capture visual and functional regression artifacts: screenshots (full-page/element), videos, traces; tune retention policies.

3. Browser & Device Matrix Mastery  
   • Configure and run Chromium, Firefox, WebKit, and Chrome/Edge channels; choose headful/headless; toggle experimental features via launch arguments.  
   • Emulate mobile devices (viewport, DPR, isMobile, user agent, orientation) and media states (prefers-color-scheme, forced-colors, reduced-motion).  
   • Understand limitations of WebKit on Windows/Linux, Firefox mobile, WebGL contexts, browser-level policies (CORS, sameSite).  
   • Manage context-level proxies, HTTP authentication, SSL errors, ignored certificate errors.  
   • Use `playwright.devices` to scale tests across device profiles and handle test "projects".

4. Network & API Layer Skills  
   • Intercept, modify and mock HTTP(S)/WebSocket traffic via `route`, `request`, `response`.  
   • Build deterministic mocks with HAR, fixtures, or inline object mocks; implement conditional mocking, dynamic overrides, pass-through, and stall scenarios.  
   • Validate API responses with Playwright’s request API (`apiRequestContext`) alongside UI steps; coordinate token sharing between API and UI contexts.

5. Performance, Stability & Scalability  
   • Discover, diagnose and eliminate flakiness: race conditions, dynamic IDs, spinners, animations, network slowness, flaky locators.  
   • Optimise execution time: parallelism (`workers`, `shard`, `retryWithWorkerId`), graceful teardown, shared fixtures, test selection, grep regex, `.only` discipline.  
   • Control resource usage on CI (CPU, RAM, container size) using browser reuse, headed run gating, selective video/tracing.  
   • Apply test retries and flaky dashboards; measure pass/fail trends, flaky counts, mean time to detection.  

6. Debugging & Observability  
   • Proficient with `pw` CLI, VS Code Playwright extension, Trace Viewer, HTML Reporter, `DEBUG=pw:api` logging, headful debugging (`--debug`, `test --ui`).  
   • Interpret trace timeline (actions, events, network, console, DOM snapshots) to root-cause issues.  
   • Use `console.log`, `page.on('console')`, `video.pause` markers to inspect runtime state.  

7. CI/CD Integration & Dev-Ops Touchpoints  
   • Integrate Playwright into GitHub Actions, Azure DevOps, GitLab CI, Jenkins, CircleCI or similar: caching browsers, reusing `npx playwright install --with-deps`, splitting shards, uploading artifacts.  
   • Configure environment variables, secrets, and service containers (databases, mock servers) in pipeline.  
   • Enforce branch gating, run smoke subset vs full suite; implement test impact analysis / selective runs.  
   • Generate JUnit/Allure/HTML/JSON reports; publish to pipelines; fail builds based on thresholds (flake count, failures).  

8. Maintainability Patterns & Code Quality  
   • Apply linting (ESLint), formatting (Prettier), typed strictness (TypeScript strict) to test code.  
   • Adhere to SOLID principles in page objects / utilities; develop helper libraries distributed via private npm packages.  
   • Participate in code reviews, propose refactors, enforce style guides, and maintain test coverage maps (statement/path coverage).  
   • Maintain version alignment with Playwright releases; plan migration when breaking changes arise; evaluate release notes.  

9. Security, Accessibility & Compliance Awareness  
   • Run basic accessibility assertions via `expect(page).toPassA11y` (with axe-core or @axcomponents), contrast checks, semantics, ARIA roles.  
   • Validate CSP, click-jacking, mixed content warnings with header expectations.  
   • Mask secrets in screenshots/traces; configure redaction in reporters; follow GDPR/PII data policies.  

10. Collaboration & Communication Outcomes  
   • Translate manual test cases or product acceptance criteria into automation tickets; estimate effort and risk.  
   • Communicate flakiness root cause to developers with actionable details.  
   • Mentor junior engineers on locators, waits, fixture design; host brown-bag sessions on Playwright features.  
   • Pair with Dev-Ops to optimise pipelines; work with Product Owners to define coverage KPIs.  

11. Tooling Ecosystem Fluency  
   • Integrate with BDD layers (Cucumber/Codecept), visual regression tools (Percy, Applitools, Loki), test management (Zephyr, Xray), and cloud browser labs (BrowserStack, Sauce, LambdaTest) via Playwright connectors.  
   • Evaluate third-party reporters (Allure, TestMo), plugins (test-retry-analyzer, test-timings).  
   • Stay aware of emerging tools like Playwright Component Testing, Playwright Experimental UI, or test generators—and assess suitability.  

12. Outcome Expectations  
   • Deliver and maintain suites that consistently execute <10 min on CI for medium-sized apps (~500 tests).  
   • Sustain <2 % flake rate over rolling 30-day window; first-time failure triage resolution ≤1 business day.  
   • Provide actionable test artefacts (trace, video, logs) with each defect ticket.  
   • Achieve ≥80 % critical path coverage for regression; author smoke subset that runs in <2 min for PRs.  
   • Contribute to quarterly roadmap to expand coverage, improve pipeline performance or adopt new Playwright versions.

An individual operating at INTERMEDIATE proficiency meets all expectations in sections 1-12: they wield Playwright confidently in day-to-day engineering, build maintainable test suites, integrate them into pipelines, and troubleshoot with minimal supervision. They may not yet architect multi-repo monorepo strategies, design global quality platforms, write custom driver patches, or evangelise advanced domain-specific abstractions—those distinguish ADVANCED and EXPERT levels—but they are fully dependable hands-on automation engineers capable of owning a mid-sized product’s end-to-end Playwright coverage.
